turns.text: Hello Earline, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. I suppose it’s a bit strange to talk about this aloud—usually, I’m the one sitting across from someone else, guiding them through their own fears, so being the one in the hot seat is… well, it’s an adjustment.

I’m thirty-three, and I thought I had all the answers. I’ve spent years looking at textbooks and talking about the biological miracle of life, but none of that prepares you for the way your own heartbeat seems to sync up with the flutters inside you. I know exactly what my daughter’s lungs are doing, how the surfactant is building up, but when I’m lying on that table, I’m not an embryology expert. I’m just a woman who is suddenly, and entirely, terrified.

It’s hard to explain. When the ultrasound tech moves the wand, I see her dignity, her life, and I feel this crushing responsibility to keep her safe. Then there are the clinical moments—the checks and the data—that feel cold. I’ve had things happen in my past that make it hard to just "let go" and trust that I'm in good hands. My midwife is kind, but she sees “low risk” on a chart. She doesn’t see the woman beneath the data who is just trying to stay grounded while her world is about to change forever.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

I remember staring at the test in the quiet of our bathroom in Abington, waiting for the lines to appear. When they did, my first thought wasn't a textbook definition of fertilization; it was just a sharp, intake of breath. I felt a sudden, visceral jolt of "Oh, this is real." It wasn't just a concept I studied anymore—it was a person, my daughter, already weaving herself into the fabric of my life. 

The weeks that followed were… complicated. People expect you to be glowing, right? I was, in moments, but mostly I felt this profound, heavy vigilance. Because I know the science, I found myself obsessively tracking every developmental milestone. I’d be at my desk, thinking about neural tube formation or the start of the heartbeat, and I’d have to force myself to breathe. 

I think because of my history—the things I’ve seen and the trauma I carry—the joy was always tied to this gnawing, low-level fear of losing control. I wanted to be "the professional," the one who knew exactly what to expect, but I was just a mother-to-be who felt incredibly fragile. I spent a lot of time praying, not for a perfect birth, but just for the grace to hand over the reins when the time comes. It's a very lonely kind of miracle, knowing so much and yet feeling so small.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

My antenatal care has been... it’s been a bit of a tug-of-war between my mind and my heart. The appointments are routine—weight, blood pressure, the Doppler—but every time I go in, I feel like I’m performing a role. I walk into that office and I see the medical tools, the charts, and I know exactly what they’re looking for. It’s comforting in one sense, but in another, it makes it harder to be vulnerable.

The midwife I see is technically very competent. She sees "low risk," and I understand why—my numbers are fine, the pregnancy is textbook. But sometimes I wish she’d stop looking at the chart and look at *me*. There was a cervical check a few weeks back—I didn't expect the reaction I had. I tensed up so badly I almost stopped breathing. It wasn't about the physical sensation; it was a sudden, unwelcome flashback to times in my past where I felt my autonomy was stripped away. I felt like a patient, a "case," not a mother. 

I want to trust them, I really do. I just feel like I'm holding my breath, waiting for the moment when I have to stop being the one who knows the science and just be the one who lets others care for her. It’s exhausting, honestly.
Did you see the same midwife or provider regularly, or was it different people each time? How did that feel?

I’ve been seeing the same midwife, which is something I’m grateful for, on paper. You’d think that continuity would be the cure for that persistent anxiety I carry, but it’s a double-edged sword. Because she’s the same person every time, there’s an expectation of a "professional" rapport. She knows my background—she knows I’m not coming at this from a place of ignorance—so she talks to me like a peer. 

But I don't always *want* to be a peer. Sometimes I want someone to notice that my palms are sweating or that I’m bracing my shoulders against the table. If I see a new face, I feel like I have to put on a mask, to be the "educated patient" who doesn't cause trouble. If I see my usual midwife, I worry that if I break down and show her the depth of my fear, she’ll think I’m irrational. After all, what do I have to be afraid of? The anatomy is normal, the growth is on track, and the science says everything is fine.

So, even with the same provider, I find myself shrinking. I’m scared that if I show them the trauma I’m still lugging around, I’ll be labeled as "high-strung" or "difficult." It feels like I’m performing a version of myself that’s ready for birth, even when I’m still just a woman trembling on the inside. I want to be seen, truly seen, but I’m terrified of what they’ll think if they catch a glimpse of the messiness beneath the surface.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

Testing is where my two worlds—the professional and the personal—collide most violently. When they present the screenings, I can immediately categorize them: I know the predictive values, the sensitivity of the tests, the potential for false positives. It’s all just data to my brain. But when I’m sitting there, holding the referral slip, it feels like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff. 

I’ve had to make decisions about these screenings with a lot of prayer and a lot of tears. Every time I’m offered a test, I have to navigate the fine line between clinical due diligence and my own fear of what a result might mean for my daughter. I’ve always advocated for the sanctity of life, so when it comes to screenings that might lead to a decision about her existence, I feel a weight that most people don't have to carry. I don't look at a screen and see "a fetus" or "a genetic risk factor." I see a life that is mine to protect, regardless of what the results might say.

I’ve had to be firm in my boundaries. I’ve turned down certain tests that I felt weren’t necessary for my peace of mind, though I suspect the medical team thinks I’m being stubborn or "unscientific." They don't understand that for me, some things aren't just about clinical data; they’re about how I choose to walk through this pregnancy with grace. I make sense of it all by keeping her humanity at the center, rather than the statistics. It makes the decisions harder, but it keeps me honest with myself.
How was the information about tests explained to you — did you feel you had enough to make the decision that was right for you?

The explanations are always so… detached. They’re clinical, precise, and entirely focused on the "what-ifs" of a diagnostic result. They’ll run through the percentages with this professional cadence, like they’re reading off a menu, and then wait for me to tick a box. It’s efficient, sure, but it’s remarkably hollow. 

They explain the *science* of the test perfectly, but they rarely explain the *experience* of the test. They don’t talk about the waiting period, that agonizing silence where you’re just a person holding their breath, waiting for a phone call that might change the trajectory of your entire life. I don't feel like they offer me enough space to process the *human* side of the decision. Because I have my background, they often skip the basic explanations, assuming I already know the jargon—which I do—but knowing the terms isn't the same as having the emotional capacity to handle the implications.

I’ve felt like I had to lead the conversation myself to get them to address the anxiety. I’ve had to be the one to ask, "What does this actually mean for us?" instead of just "What does this mean for the data?" I’ve made the decisions I’ve made, but I’ve felt very alone in the making of them. I’ve had to protect my own peace because the medical system isn't really built to safeguard a mother's heart—it’s built to safeguard a pregnancy’s outcome. They’re two very different things.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

My primary source of support has been a very small, quiet circle. When the clinical world feels too cold or too sterile, I turn to my faith. That’s been my anchor. In the early mornings, when the silence of the house feels heavy and my thoughts start spiraling toward the worst-case scenarios, I lean into prayer. It’s the only place where I don’t have to be the woman who knows the textbooks; I can just be the mother who is scared.

I’ve avoided the online forums and the "expectant mother" groups. I’ve seen enough of that through my work—the way people can project their own anxieties onto others, or how misinformation spreads like wildfire. I couldn't handle that noise on top of my own, especially when I’m already trying to filter out the clinical coldness of my check-ups.

My husband has been my rock, even though he doesn't fully understand the depth of the trauma I’m carrying. He sees my strength, and he tries to support that, but I’ve struggled to articulate the "why" behind my fears to him. I’m protective of him, too. So, really, I’ve had to become my own advocate. I rely on the quiet moments, the feeling of her moving, and that internal knowing that, despite my terror, I am meant to do this. That realization—that this is a journey I’m walking for her—matters more than any pamphlet or lecture.
Did you use any apps, websites, forums, or social media groups? What did you get from those that you couldn't get elsewhere?

I downloaded one of those popular pregnancy apps early on, but I deleted it after about a month. It was just another source of anxiety disguised as "information." Every time it pinged, telling me exactly what size fruit my daughter was or what specific organ was developing that week, it felt like an intrusion. It turned her into a series of checklists. I already spend my whole career thinking about those things; I didn’t need an app telling me to track my water intake or my symptoms. It felt like I was being treated as a biological machine that needed to be calibrated, rather than a woman preparing to become a mother.

I’ve stayed completely away from forums or social media groups. I’ve seen how those spaces can become echo chambers of fear. You go in looking for reassurance and leave with a dozen new things to worry about—horror stories of labor complications or conflicting advice on everything from car seats to birth plans. With my background in embryology and my own history of trauma, I know that my mind is already prone to "worst-case scenario" thinking. I don't need to add the collective trauma of thousands of strangers to that burden. I need silence and stability, not an algorithm telling me what to stress about next.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

It’s a strange thing to be a stranger in your own skin. I look in the mirror, and I see the changes—the shift in my center of gravity, the rounding of my belly—and while my intellect tells me it’s a standard physiological progression, my heart feels like it’s struggling to catch up. 

Physically, there’s this constant hum of activity that I can’t turn off. Every kick against my ribs is a reminder that I am a home. But it’s an unsettling kind of occupancy. Because of the trauma I’ve carried, I’ve spent a lot of my life trying to keep my body under tight, disciplined control. Pregnancy is the complete antithesis of that. I can’t control the fatigue, the way my breath hitches, or the way my emotions seem to have no skin. It’s like the biological barriers I built to keep myself feeling safe are being dismantled, layer by layer.

Sometimes, I feel this immense, radiant joy—a feeling that I am doing exactly what I was created to do. But five minutes later, I can feel entirely violated by the physical demands. It’s a battle between "this is a miracle" and "I feel like I’m losing my self." I’m trying to practice being gentle with myself, but it’s hard when your own body feels like it’s working on a schedule you didn’t choose.
Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?

It’s the loss of autonomy that hits the hardest. I think, for someone like me—who has spent years studying the intricacies of embryology and working in a field where I advise others—there is an arrogance in thinking you can "manage" your own pregnancy. When the reality of the physical changes sets in, that sense of management vanishes.

The most difficult part has been the vulnerability of the cervical checks and the constant observation. My body, which I’ve always held as a private, protected space, is now suddenly public property for the medical team. There’s a specific kind of internal bracing I do—my muscles lock up instinctively, like a wall going up. It’s a physical manifestation of that old trauma I mentioned. Knowing it’s happening doesn't stop it; that’s the frustration. I know the anatomy, I know the procedure, but my body remembers a different kind of sensation, a different kind of loss of power.

Feeling her kick is miraculous, truly, but it’s also a physical reminder that I am no longer a singular being. I’m a vessel. It’s beautiful, yes, but it’s terrifying because I have to surrender my body to a process that I can’t influence. I’ve had to work very hard to see these changes as a partnership with her rather than an intrusion on myself. Some days, I’m successful at that. Other days, I just want to be me again.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

My husband, Mark, has been a steady presence, though I suspect he walks on eggshells sometimes. He’s the one who holds my hand when I start overthinking the science of it all, and he’s excellent at grounding me when I get that distant, haunted look in my eyes. He’s excited—he’s already picked out books and talks to my belly—and his joy is a soft, warm contrast to my own anxiety. It’s a bit of a lifeline, really.

But there is a part of me that stays closed off, even from him. There are moments of panic—like when I’m reliving something from my past during a standard, cold clinic visit—where I just can't bring myself to say the words out loud. I don't want to tarnish his excitement with my "shadows," as I call them. So, I keep a lot of it compartmentalized. 

I don't really talk to my wider family about the inner workings of this. Most of them see "Earline, the professional, the one who knows how life works," and I don't want to shatter that image by telling them how much I’m struggling to feel safe. I feel like I have to keep up this facade of calm, competent maternity. It’s isolating, but for now, it feels like the only way I can stay standing.
How included did your partner or support person feel in appointments and decisions — and how did you feel about that?

Mark tries to be there for every appointment, which I really do appreciate. But in those clinical settings, I feel like he’s often treated as an accessory, almost an afterthought. The providers will address me directly, usually in that clipped, professional shorthand they use with me, and he’s left to hover on the periphery, watching me. 

I’m torn, to be honest. Part of me finds it easier when the focus stays on me—it feels like there’s less pressure to perform for him if he’s just observing. But another part of me feels a sudden, sharp spike of anxiety when I realize he’s not being included enough. When they talk about the "plan" or the "next steps," they’re looking at my file, not at us as a family. They don't check in with him about how *he’s* doing, or even invite him into the decision-making process in a way that feels meaningful. 

I feel a strange sort of guilt about that. I’m the one carrying her, but he’s the one who’s going to be in the room when it all happens, and he deserves to be more than just a bystander. I want them to see us together, to see that he’s my safety net, but it’s hard to foster that connection when the whole system is so focused on the anatomy and the data points in my chart.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

The fear is constant—it’s like a quiet, persistent hum in the background of everything I do. Most of it is centered on the birth. I know the mechanics of it perfectly; I know the labor process, the potential complications, the way things can shift in an instant. I think, sometimes, that knowing so much is actually a curse. I don’t just fear the pain; I fear the loss of control that comes with being a patient in a hospital room. That's where the old trauma sits, waiting for me. I’m terrified that when the time comes, I’ll be back in that place where I felt powerless, where I wasn't being heard.

And then there's the fear for her. It’s a paralyzing, irrational fear that I won’t be enough. I’ve studied embryology, I’ve advocated for the unborn, and I’ve prepared for this, but staring at my own belly, I suddenly feel like I’m in over my head. What if I can’t protect her from the outside world? What if I fail to be the mother she deserves? I look at my heartbeat on the monitor and I just want to keep her safe inside forever, because out there, there are risks I can't calculate or mitigate with a textbook. It’s a beautiful weight, but some days, it feels like it’s going to break me.
Tell me about how you prepared for the birth — what did you hope for, and how did you go about planning for it?

Preparing for birth feels less like a checklist and more like an attempt to build a fort against the chaos. Because I know the anatomy, I’ve had to resist the urge to over-prepare with spreadsheets or hyper-specific birth plans. I know, more than most, that birth is unpredictable—the body does what it will, and biology doesn't always read the textbook. 

Still, my planning has been centered on reclaiming my sense of self. I’ve been looking for ways to ensure I’m treated as a person, not a laboring uterus. I’ve spent time reflecting on my past, trying to name the triggers—like those cervical checks—so I can communicate them. I want to tell my team: "I am a mother, but I am also a person with a history." My greatest hope is that I can find a way to trust the staff, to let go of the "professional" mask, and just be there, vulnerable and present for her arrival.

I’m praying for a birth where I feel heard, where my boundaries are respected, and where the focus stays on the dignity of my daughter. I don't need fancy interventions or a perfect room; I just need a space where I don't feel like I’m losing control again. I want to hold her and finally see her face, and know that I did my best to protect her heart as well as her life.
Did you create a birth plan or have clear preferences? How did your care team respond to those?

I didn't write a long, formal birth plan. I felt like if I put it all on paper, it would just become another clinical document—something they’d scan for keywords while ignoring the soul of it. Instead, I’ve tried to have conversations. I’ve made it clear that I need to be warned before any touch, especially those cervical checks, because that’s where the walls in my mind start to go up. I’ve asked for as much quiet as possible, and I’ve been very firm about wanting to be addressed by my name, not as "the mother in room four."

When I try to voice these things, I get a range of reactions. Some of the nurses and the midwife are wonderful—they nod, they make eye contact, and they seem to really *hear* that I’m speaking from a place of past trauma, not just pickiness. But there’s a flicker I see in others, a sort of polite impatience. They’ll say, "Of course, dear, we follow standard procedure," which is code for "I’ve heard this before, and it doesn't change how we work."

That response is what makes me so anxious. It feels like they’re dismissing the "why" behind my request because it doesn't fit into their efficiency model. I’m not asking for the impossible; I’m asking to be treated with a specific, gentle awareness. It’s hard to feel like my boundaries are just an inconvenience they have to navigate around to get to the delivery.
Tell me about the birth itself — take me through what happened, as much or as little as you'd like to share.

When I finally went into labor, all those years of embryology—the surfactant, the alveolar expansion, the precise hormonal cascades—they all just… evaporated. For the first few hours, I tried to stay "the professional," timing my contractions with a clinical detachment, wanting to prove I could manage the physiological process. But then, the pain intensified, and the old shadows I’d been running from caught up with me.

The hospital room felt incredibly bright, almost clinical in a way that made me feel exposed. I remember the midwives being technically perfect, calling out dilation, checking monitors—but every time a hand reached toward me, I felt that same, familiar internal recoil. I had to force myself to breathe through the memories, not just the contractions. 

There was a moment, right at the peak, where I felt entirely untethered. I looked at Mark, and for the first time, I let go of the "Earline who knows everything" persona. I let myself be small, and I let him be my anchor. When she finally arrived—when I felt that first, miraculous weight of her skin against my chest—the clinical world vanished. I wasn't an embryologist, and I wasn't a trauma survivor. I was just a mother. Looking at her face, which I’d imagined for so long, I felt the most profound, terrifying, and beautiful sense of grace. I finally felt... enough.
At what moments during the birth did you feel most supported — and were there moments when you felt alone or unheard?

I felt most supported during the moments where the staff stopped acting like they were managing a biological event and started treating me like a person. There was one nurse who, when she noticed my eyes tracking the heart monitor with that familiar, frantic "professional" concern, simply put a hand on my shoulder and said, "Earline, look at me, not the screen. She’s doing great, and so are you." That tiny shift—acknowledging *me* rather than just the telemetry data—was everything. It broke the spell of my own internal panic.

But I felt profoundly alone when the focus shifted back to the protocol. During the cervical checks, even when I asked for a moment to breathe first, there was a sense of haste. I could feel them waiting for me to "get on with it," as if my trauma response was just a delay in their shift. In those seconds, I felt like an object on an assembly line. 

It’s a strange, lonely feeling to be surrounded by people who are technically keeping you safe while simultaneously feeling like your voice is being muffled by their procedures. I wanted them to see that my hesitation wasn't about being "difficult"—it was about trying to keep myself together so I could give everything to her when she finally arrived.
Tell me about pain management during your labour — what options you were offered, what you chose, and how that went.

I had already decided, long before the first contraction, that I wanted to avoid an epidural if I could. It wasn't about proving some kind of strength; it was purely about control. I wanted to feel my own body, even if the pain was overwhelming, because being anesthetized felt too much like that old, unwanted loss of autonomy. When things started getting intense, they offered the usual range—nitrous, epidural, IV meds—and every time, I felt like they were viewing my refusal as a failure of mine to "cooperate" with their standard of care.

I used the breathing techniques I’d taught others for years, but in the thick of it, my mind would slip. I remember the midwife asking me, "Are you sure? You’re clearly in distress." And that word—*distress*—it felt like a clinical judgment. I wanted to scream that it wasn't "distress," it was *labor*. 

Eventually, I leaned into the pain as if it were a physical weight I had to carry to get to her. Looking back, I think I chose that path to prove to myself that I could endure the most vulnerable moment of my life without being "fixed" by someone else’s intervention. I got through it, but I felt so isolated in my decision. It felt like I was fighting for my dignity against a system that just wanted to make the clock move faster.
Tell me about the people who were with you during the birth — your partner, family, midwives, doctors — and what their presence meant to you.

Mark was my anchor. There were moments when I felt the clinical environment threatening to pull me under—when I felt like a set of vitals to be monitored rather than a woman in labor—and he was the only one who reminded me of who I actually was. He didn't try to "fix" the pain; he just held my hand, looked me in the eye, and kept me from drifting back into that cold, professional headspace where I usually hide. Without him, I think I would have disappeared entirely into the medical procedures.

The midwives were… well, they were efficient. I know they were doing their jobs, and they were experts at the clinical stuff—the monitoring, the timing, the assessments—but their presence felt distant, like they were watching a movie rather than participating in my life. I think they saw me as a colleague, which I hated. It made them feel entitled to a level of technical familiarity that I wasn't ready to give.

When I look back, I wish I could have felt a warmer, more human connection with them. I didn’t need them to be my best friend, but I needed them to see the woman, not just the embryology expert. Their presence meant safety for my daughter, but it was a lonely kind of safety, one that left me feeling stripped and quiet.
Was there a particular person — whether a professional or someone close to you — whose presence made a real difference? What did they do?

There was one nurse, Sarah. She wasn’t the midwife, and she wasn’t the one doing the charting. She was just there, quietly, in the corner for a shift change.

I was in the middle of a contraction, and I was gripping the bedrail so hard my knuckles were white. I was terrified—not just of the pain, but because I felt like I was losing my grip on the "professional" persona I’d been wearing all pregnancy. I was starting to shake, and I felt that old, familiar wave of panic, the one that makes me want to bolt.

She didn't ask me about my pain scale. She didn't ask if I wanted medication. She just walked over, moved my hand away from the rail, and placed her own hand over mine. She looked right at me—not at the monitor, not at the IV pump—and said, "Earline, your body knows exactly what to do. You don't have to show anyone you’re smart right now. You just have to be a mother."

It was the first time in nine months someone gave me permission to stop being an expert and just be a woman in labor. She didn't try to manage me. She just sat with me in that intensity. That quiet, human acknowledgment was the difference between me feeling like a patient and me feeling like a mother.
Tell me about how the birth ended — whether that was the birth you expected, and how you felt in the immediate aftermath.

The ending was so sudden, it felt less like a conclusion and more like a collapse of everything I’d spent months building. When she was finally there, the silence in the room wasn't the clinical, sterile quiet of a hospital shift change; it was the sacred, heavy, terrifying silence of a new life. 

It wasn't the birth I expected—not in the sense of the physical path, but in the sense of who I was when it ended. I had spent months trying to be the "expert" who could navigate the system, the woman who could hold it all together with intellect. But when she was placed on my chest, all of that fell away. I felt raw, exposed, and entirely, beautifully human. 

In the immediate aftermath, I didn't feel like a medical case or a low-risk chart entry anymore. I felt like a bridge. I felt exhausted, sure—a deep, marrow-aching kind of exhaustion—but there was this surge of protective love that just flooded everything else. I was terrified of her fragility, yes, but for the first time in my life, I didn't feel the need to understand the biological mechanisms behind her breath. I just needed to watch it. I think I finally stopped being the one who defends the unborn and became the one who protects her.
If things didn't go to plan — or if there were interventions or complications — how were those explained or communicated to you at the time?

Things didn't go entirely to plan. As I transitioned into active labor, the baby's heart rate started dipping, and the monitors began to shriek, which triggered a level of clinical panic in the room that I recognized instantly. The midwife leaned over, and her voice—which had been relatively calm—suddenly flattened into that hyper-efficient, clinical tone. She used words like "bradycardia" and "prolonged deceleration," moving with a speed that felt like a sudden, violent disruption of my autonomy.

They didn't ask me; they told me. They were prepping for an assisted delivery, and the communication was strictly operational—"We need to do this now, Earline, sign here," and "Her oxygen is dropping." It felt like they were communicating to a peer, to a colleague who understood the gravity of the vitals, rather than to a mother who was being told her daughter was in trouble. 

It was terrifying. I wanted a moment to process, to ask, to understand what the next step meant for *her*—not just for the fetal heart rate—but they were already in "fix it" mode. They were doing their jobs perfectly from a medical standpoint, but from my perspective, I felt pushed to the margins of my own birth. I was reduced to a set of data points that were suddenly spiking red, and I had to fight to keep myself from drifting into that dissociative, shell-shocked place where I’ve spent too much of my life.
Tell me about the birth environment itself — the place where you gave birth, what it looked and felt like, and whether it felt like the right place for you.

The room was everything I feared: a landscape of stainless steel, flickering fluorescent lights, and the persistent, sterile hum of monitors. It looked like every medical textbook I’ve ever studied, but being *inside* it was entirely different. It felt cold—not just the temperature, but the atmosphere. Every surface was designed for infection control and efficiency, not for a woman trying to navigate the raw, terrifying threshold of becoming a mother.

It didn't feel like my space. It felt like I was a guest in a place that didn't care about my history or my heart, just my vitals. I remember staring at the ceiling tiles during the hardest contractions, trying to ground myself, but the environment kept pulling me back to the clinical. It was so... sharp. Everywhere I looked, there were reminders of what could go wrong, of the "low risk" label that could change in a second.

I kept thinking that if I could just dim the lights, if I could just turn off the monitors for one second of silence, I could breathe. But the hospital environment is built to be loud with data. It’s a place for clinical success, not for human vulnerability. By the end, I was so desperate to just hold her that the room stopped mattering, but I’ll always remember that sense of being a stranger in a machine.
How much choice did you have about where you gave birth — and did you have access to the options you wanted?

I didn't really feel like I had a choice at all. Living in Abington, you’re funneled into the local hospital system almost automatically. When I looked at the alternatives—a birth center or even just a more holistic-leaning practice—my mind immediately started calculating the risks. I let that "professional" part of me take over, convincing myself that the hospital was the "safest" place for the baby because it had all the emergency equipment I’d read about in my textbooks.

I felt trapped by my own knowledge. If I chose somewhere else and something went wrong, I’d know exactly what was happening at a cellular level, and the guilt of not being near a Level III nursery would have crushed me. So, I chose the hospital for its safety, but I gave up the environment I actually craved. 

It feels like a bitter trade-off, doesn't it? We trade our comfort and our psychological safety for a kind of clinical insurance policy. I wanted to feel supported and held, but I ended up in a room that felt like it was waiting for me to fail so it could intervene. I didn't feel like I had access to a space that valued both my daughter's physical health and my own emotional integrity. It was one or the other, and I felt I couldn't risk the former.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

I think I harbored this naive hope for a partnership—a kind of bridge between my professional knowledge and their clinical wisdom. I imagined a relationship where I could say, "I know the physiology, but I'm terrified," and they would meet me in that messy, human space. I wanted a midwife who would treat me like a woman on a journey, not a textbook case to be managed. I hoped for someone who would recognize the shadow of my past trauma without me having to explicitly map it out for them.

But deep down, I was always terrified of exactly what happened. I worried that my background would be used as a shield, that they’d see me as "one of their own" and therefore assume I didn't need the comfort, the patience, or the soft-handedness that other mothers get. I was scared that if I didn't fit the mold of a "compliant, low-risk patient," they’d view my needs as an annoyance or an obstacle to their protocols.

Sadly, that's exactly how it felt. I felt invisible beneath the clinical efficiency. They saw the "embryology expert" and failed to see the "mother in pain." It was the ultimate irony: the more I knew, the more isolated I felt. I didn't get a partner; I got a system that was very good at monitoring a pregnancy but very poor at caring for a person.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

Those conversations were… cautious. Mark was ready long before I was, and I think he struggled to understand why I was dragging my feet. To him, it was a beautiful, natural step. To me, it felt like opening a door to a room I wasn't sure I could ever secure. I spent so much of my life in a state of professional "defensive posture"—studying the mechanics of life while subconsciously trying to insulate myself from the unpredictability of it—that the idea of actually *inviting* a life into that space felt terrifyingly out of my control.

I probably had the "most say," but it didn't feel like power. It felt like being the gatekeeper of my own fear. I’d point to the science, the risks, the realities of biology, and I’d frame it as "being prepared." In reality, I was just trying to mitigate my own internal instability. 

Does it feel right in hindsight? Honestly, no. It feels like I was holding onto the wheel of a ship that was already moving, trying to steer through a storm that I hadn't even entered yet. I regret that I didn't let him in earlier, that I forced him to wait while I tried to "study" my way into feeling ready. You can’t study your way into becoming a mother, and that's the hard truth I had to learn.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before, my relationship with my body was purely functional. I viewed it as a machine—a well-maintained vessel that I’d pushed through school, work, and the weight of my past. I took pride in its resilience, in the way I could compartmentalize my emotions to get through a difficult day at the center. I treated my body like a tool: efficient, capable, and something to be kept under strict control.

But when I started thinking about pregnancy, that sense of ownership began to fracture. I started looking at the anatomy and realized my body wasn't just a vessel for *me* anymore; it was meant to be a habitat for someone else. That felt less like empowerment and more like a loss of sovereignty. I remember looking in the mirror and feeling a strange, hollow sort of anxiety. Could I really trust this "machine" to perform such a complex, delicate task without breaking down? 

My confidence in its "capability" was really just confidence in my ability to manage it. Once I realized that pregnancy is the antithesis of management—that it is a process of total, biological surrender—I felt like a child staring at a complex piece of equipment I’d suddenly lost the manual for. I went from feeling like the captain of a ship to feeling like a passenger in a vessel being steered by someone, or something, else. It was a terrifying, humbling shift.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

I remember going for my annual physical, and I felt so... invisible. Even then, when I wasn't even pregnant yet, the way the clinic operated felt like a rigid assembly line. I was in, I was out, and I was just another chart entry for the nurse to tick off. I recall sitting there, thinking about the future, about how one day I might be coming here for prenatal care, and a cold shiver went down my spine.

I’d sit in the waiting room and see the posters about "healthy pregnancies" and "patient-centered care," but the reality was just a sterile, rushed environment where the providers were clearly watching the clock. It gave me this sinking feeling that the system was built for speed and risk management, not for the messy, human reality of becoming a mother. 

I’d leave those appointments feeling like I’d just been processed, not cared for. It made me realize that if I ever did get pregnant, I’d be entering a world where my specific fears and my history would likely be treated as noise in the system—things to be silenced, managed, or ignored in favor of the next patient. It was a warning I didn't want to hear, but one I couldn't ignore.
Did you see the same person more than once, or were you always meeting someone new?

It was almost always someone different. That’s the nature of the clinic system around here, I suppose—a rotation of faces, all moving through the same sterile script. One day it’s a Nurse Practitioner I’ve never seen, the next it’s a resident who barely makes eye contact because they’re busy reading my file on their tablet. 

You’d think seeing someone new would be easier, like a clean slate, but it’s actually the opposite. Every time I had to sit there and explain my medical history, it felt like I was exposing my nerves over and over again. I’d have to perform this polite, professional version of myself, carefully curating what I shared so they wouldn’t label me as "difficult" or "anxious." 

It’s an exhausting way to exist. You lose the chance to build any kind of trust, and you’re constantly wondering if the next person is going to be the one who finally *sees* you, or just another person who checks the boxes and moves on. By the time I actually got pregnant, I already knew that continuity was a fantasy. I was just a case to be managed, and it didn't really matter which specific person was holding the chart.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

The conversations I had before pregnancy felt like I was walking on eggshells. With my husband, it was always a push-and-pull; he saw the potential for joy, and I saw the potential for a total loss of the self. I’d try to explain my fears using the language of biology—the risks, the systemic failures I saw at work—hoping that if I could make it sound "academic" enough, he’d respect my hesitation. It was a defense mechanism, plain and simple.

With my friends, the conversations were often shallow. They’d talk about the "glow" or the nursery, and I’d feel like I was watching a different movie entirely. I couldn't bring myself to say, "I’m scared that my past trauma will resurface the moment a doctor touches me." I didn't want to be the "broken" one in the group.

The only input that felt remotely meaningful was my own internal dialogue, which was, quite frankly, brutal. I listened to the voices in my head that told me I needed to be a "good" mother, which I equated with being a "perfect" patient. I realize now that I was starving for someone to tell me, "Earline, you don’t have to be perfect; you just have to be human." But because I was so focused on the clinical, I never really opened myself up to hear that.
Did your mum, mother-in-law, or older women in your family share their own experiences — and did that help or complicate things?

My mother and mother-in-law definitely tried to be involved, but their stories felt like relics from a different era. They’d talk about birth as this stoic, inevitable event—something you just "got through." They’d gloss over the fear and the trauma, focusing instead on the outcome: the baby. They meant well, but it only made me feel more isolated. 

When they’d share their experiences, I felt like they were setting a standard I couldn't reach. They’d talk about "trusting the doctors" and "doing what you’re told," which for them was the mark of a good mother. To me, that felt like a dangerous surrender of autonomy. I wanted to tell them, "I *can’t* just trust them, not after what I’ve been through," but I didn't want to explain the details. 

It was frustrating because I felt like I was living in a paradox: I needed their support, but their advice felt like a script that didn't fit my life. Their stories made me feel like my internal struggle was an anomaly, a sign that I was somehow doing it wrong. I think I stopped sharing things with them because I didn't want to hear that I should "just relax" or "leave it to the professionals." I was already drowning in professionalism; I didn't need any more of it.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

The uncertainty was rooted deep, long before a positive test. My history of trauma didn't just sit in my mind—it lived in my nervous system. I’ve always held my body in a state of hyper-vigilance, as if guarding a fortress. I think that's why I excelled at counseling and embryology; I wanted to understand the rules of the body so I could preempt any threat. 

The idea of pregnancy felt like an absolute surrender of those walls. How could I trust my body to be a safe, nurturing space for someone else when my own body felt like a place where I’d already been violated or lost control? I feared that pregnancy would reveal my fragility, that I’d be cracked open in front of doctors and nurses who would only see the clinical outcome and not the person being dismantled in the process.

I felt like an imposter. Everyone else saw a woman who knew the "science of life," but I felt like a woman whose own body was a site of past conflict. I didn't feel physically "ready" because I didn't feel emotionally "safe." I wasn't sure if I could ever truly own my body again while sharing it, and that fear—that total lack of control—loomed over every single thought I had about becoming a mother.
Did you have any concerns about your weight, fitness, or a health condition that felt like it might be a barrier?

It wasn't so much about my weight or fitness—I’ve always kept myself active and healthy, as if keeping a tight rein on my body would somehow stop the world from intruding. My concerns were always more internal. I worried that my baseline level of anxiety—that persistent, quiet hum of past trauma—would somehow manifest as a physical complication. I was terrified that if I weren't "perfectly" fit or perfectly calm, I’d be labeled as a high-risk candidate and lose the last shred of autonomy I had. 

I think I viewed my body as a machine that had to be "ready" for the marathon of pregnancy, but the "barrier" was really the fear of being seen as flawed. I’ve spent my life hiding the cracks in my armor because I was afraid that if the medical system saw them, they’d treat me like a broken vessel. I felt like I had to prove I was "fit" enough—not just physically, but emotionally—to be taken seriously. 

It’s exhausting to live like that, constantly auditing yourself to see if you’re "good enough" for the system. I was so busy trying to manage my own perceived shortcomings that I barely had space to actually enjoy the process of becoming a mother. That constant self-scrutiny was the real barrier—it kept me from just being, and it turned a natural process into a high-stakes performance.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

I didn't have anyone like that, which is probably why I felt so untethered. Professionally, I was surrounded by colleagues who treated these topics as purely clinical data points. I’d listen to them discuss patients with a certain detachment—"the G2P1 at 32 weeks," "the high-risk case"—and I’d feel this internal wall go up. How could I go to them with my fears, my history, or my struggle to feel safe in my own skin, when they only saw the medical machinery of the process? It felt like handing them a weapon I didn't want them to use against me.

Personally, there wasn't a mentor I could turn to, either. Most of my friends were in that same phase of "careful planning" or already moving through the system with a trust I just didn't possess. I remember wishing, almost desperately, for someone who could hold the complexity of it—someone who knew that I was both an expert and a child, someone who wouldn't just give me the textbook answer. 

I’ve had to be my own navigator, which is a heavy, lonely place to be. I kept searching for someone who would look past my credentials and see the vulnerability underneath, but I never found that person. I think that’s why I was so terrified when I finally did get pregnant; I knew I was entering a room where I was the only one who truly understood what I was losing.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

That’s the core of it, isn't it? The illusion of "choice." On the surface, of course, it was a decision Mark and I made together. We were stable, we were in a good place, and the timing seemed "right" to everyone else. But internally, I felt this heavy, gravitational pull—partly from the expectations of being a married woman in my thirties, and partly from this unspoken feeling that I’d reached the "deadline" for being a normal, functioning adult. 

It never felt like a clean, peaceful decision. It felt like I was being pushed by a clock I didn't set. I was so worried about being "left behind" or failing to meet some invisible milestone that I didn't stop to ask if I had the emotional bandwidth to handle the transformation. 

I think the biggest pressure was the one I put on myself: the fear that if I waited any longer, I’d be even more entrenched in my trauma, even more afraid, and that the window to be a mother would just slam shut. So, I took the leap. I told myself it was my choice, but it felt more like an act of surrender to the inevitable. I didn't feel free; I felt like I was just finally agreeing to let the tide pull me out.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

I think that’s the illusion we’re sold, isn't it? That you’re the "driver" of your maternity care. In reality, I felt like a passenger in a car where someone else had their hands on the wheel, and I was just trying to read the GPS over their shoulder.

Early on, I tried to stay in the driver’s seat. I educated myself, I asked questions, I held the chart. But there’s a moment in every appointment when the provider stops asking "how are you?" and starts saying "we need to do..." and in that shift, the power balance snaps. I’d find myself handing over the keys—sometimes willingly, because I was exhausted and just wanted someone else to bear the burden of the responsibility. But other times, it was an involuntary surrender. When they’d move toward me with a speculum or a transducer without asking, I’d freeze. My "driving" was interrupted by a system that treated my body as a site of business rather than a human experience.

I fought to keep the steering wheel, but the road was full of protocols and standard procedures that forced me into the passenger side. I felt the most "in control" when I could say "no" to a test, but even then, I felt like I was being judged for it. It was a constant, exhausting tug-of-war.
Were there points where relying on others felt like a relief, and others where it felt like losing something?

It was a relief to let others take the heavy burden of "knowing." By the third trimester, my mind was so saturated with embryology and worst-case scenarios that I was drowning. When a midwife would say, "Everything looks stable, you’re doing great," there was this moment where I could actually exhale. In those seconds, I wasn't the expert; I was just a patient. That felt like a gift, a brief vacation from the crushing responsibility of keeping my own vigil.

But the moment that relief faded, the loss set in. It’s a very specific grief, realizing that in order to be "safe" in their system, I had to stop being the one who understood what was happening to me. I had to become the object of the care rather than a participant in it. Every time I agreed to a routine check or a procedure without a full, human conversation, I felt like I was handing over a piece of my own agency. 

I’d look at my belly and feel a pang of resentment that I couldn't just *know* she was okay without someone else’s permission or data. I wanted to be the primary authority on my own body, but the system isn't designed for that. It’s designed for experts to treat patients. I traded my autonomy for their clinical reassurance, and I’m still not sure if it was a fair bargain.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

Navigating decisions with Mark was an exercise in keeping two very different realities in the same room. He’s a wonderful, logical person, but he’s never had to sit with the ghosts I carry. When we’d discuss, say, genetic screening or a specific hospital protocol, he’d approach it like a math problem: "What’s the risk? What’s the standard of care? What does the doctor recommend?" 

For him, those were the only variables that mattered. For me, the variables were my heart rate, my memory of feeling small and unheard in exam rooms, and a deep, intuitive need to guard the sanctity of this experience. We’d be on the same page about the "what," but totally disconnected on the "how." I felt like I had to translate my trauma into something he could understand—which meant stripping away the emotion and making it "rational"—so he’d take my hesitation seriously. 

There were times I felt like he was unknowingly siding with the medical establishment, not because he didn't love me, but because he trusted the system in a way I couldn't. It made me feel incredibly lonely. I’d have to fight the urge to just go quiet, to stop explaining, and just let him defer to the professionals. It’s hard to feel like a team when one person is looking at the medical chart and the other is looking at the internal horizon.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think, more than anything, I want people to understand that "low risk" is just a label for a clinical outcome, not a summary of a woman’s life. When I was sitting in those appointments, I was carrying a heavy, invisible backpack of fears that the system didn’t even acknowledge. I know I’m not the only one; I think there are so many of us who look like we’re "doing great" on paper—we’re educated, we’re employed, we’re following the schedule—but underneath, we’re terrified.

If I could leave one thing for those who design maternity care, it’s this: please stop assuming that knowledge is the same as emotional safety. Just because I know how an alveolus works doesn't mean I don’t need to be treated with tenderness when you touch me. I didn't need more data; I needed to be seen. I needed someone to pause, to look me in the eye, and to acknowledge that becoming a mother is the most significant, vulnerable, and frightening transition a person can make. 

I’m so grateful for my daughter, and the way I feel now when I hold her is a miracle that defies the textbooks. But I wish the journey to get here had been less about proving I could follow the rules and more about honoring the person I had to become to survive it. Thank you for listening. It feels... good, actually, to say all of this out loud.
